sections.title: Integrating legal and ethical checks
sections.markdown:
We check for compliance before tests that could be sensitive: price displays, health claims, or manipulative messaging. We avoid deceptive urgency. If a test uses social proof (counts of users), ensure counts are accurate. Ethical constraints narrow the space of testable variants, and that's appropriate.

One more micro‑scene: social ad copy test
We need clicks at a fixed CPA. We test two ad copies on a small budget: control with benefit text vs. variant with a question. We allocate equal daily budgets for 3 days and check cost per click and cost per conversion. We predefine the acceptable CPA. This adds the constraint of budget into the decision rules.

We assumed the question style would lower CPA → observed CPC dropped 10% but conversion rate also fell → net CPA rose 8% → we paused variant and designed a third variant blending clarity and curiosity. The pivot shows how cost constraints shape experiments.

sections.title: How to deal with conflicting metrics
sections.markdown:
Sometimes tests show mixed signals: clicks up, revenue per click down. We use a decision matrix:

- If primary business metric improves, accept unless secondary harm is severe.
- If primary improves but secondary shows small harm, consider a constrained rollout with monitoring.
- If primary improves but the change is ethically questionable, reject.

We choose primary metrics carefully because they govern decisions.

sections.markdown:

Misconceptions revisited: p‑values and practical decisions
We acknowledge statistical formalities but focus on action. P‑values are tools; they do not replace judgment. A p‑value <0.05 gives some confidence, but we prefer combining p‑values with practical effect sizes and implementation costs. When p‑values disagree with the business case, choose the path that balances risk and upside: deploy small, reversible changes and monitor.

sections.title: Scaling learning across teams
sections.markdown:
When experiments multiply, we categorize learnings by domain: email, landing pages, pricing, ads. Each domain has different typical effect sizes and costs. We share short case notes across teams: "subject line pattern A improved opens by median 12% across three tests."

We also avoid duplication: maintain an experiment registry in Brali LifeOS to prevent running similar tests in parallel on the same cohort.

sections.title: Final friction points and how we remove them
sections.markdown:
The most common friction is inertia — starting a test. We remove it by lowering the activation cost: a one‑line hypothesis in Brali, a pre‑filled template, and a 5‑minute rule. The second friction is premature enthusiasm; we remove it by predefining stopping rules.

We practiced these changes: we assumed quick wins would compound automatically → observed inconsistent follow‑through → changed to a registry and weekly 10‑minute review. That small operational change increased test delivery by 40% in the next quarter.

sections.title: How to learn faster with constrained traffic
sections.markdown:
If traffic is limited, do one of three things:

- Test more impactful elements (price, offer, major headline).
- Use within‑user experiments (A/B test parts of onboarding where each user sees both versions in sequence, with random order).
- Pool traffic by running tests across multiple channels simultaneously and checking for channel interactions.

We often combine these: start with a high‑impact test and route paid traffic to accelerate sample collection.

howTo.title: Define the metric baseline
howTo.summary: Identify the current performance metrics you want to improve, such as open rates or conversion rates. Establish a clear baseline to measure the impact of your A/B tests.

howTo.title: Create a test hypothesis
howTo.summary: Formulate a simple, testable hypothesis that outlines the change you will make and the expected outcome. Keep it concise and focused on one variable.

howTo.title: Run the 3-day experiment
howTo.summary: Implement your A/B test by splitting your audience and running the experiment for a predetermined duration. Ensure you track both primary and secondary metrics throughout the test.

howTo.title: Analyze the results
howTo.summary: After the experiment concludes, review the data to determine if your hypothesis was validated. Look for significant changes in the primary metric compared to the baseline.

howTo.title: Check in with LifeOS Brali app
howTo.summary: Log your results and insights in the Brali LifeOS app to capture learnings and inform future tests. Access it at https://metalhatscats.com/life-os/ab-test-learning-loop/.
